  "opinion": "As a woman raised in a family where education wasn't prioritized—my father dropped out of school after primary and my mother never finished high school—I've always felt the weight of expectations that I should be 'better' than others to succeed. Stereotype threat in STEM isn't just a theoretical concept; it's something I've seen play out in my own life. When I try to explain my work in sales to my kids, they often ask why I can't do math like their teachers, which makes me anxious because I know that stereotype can hold people back from even trying. In Bologna, where I live with my three children and my partner, I've noticed how many immigrant families struggle with the same thing: they fear their children will be labeled as 'not smart enough' in STEM fields because of their background. This is especially true for me as a Muslim woman who grew up in a country where education wasn't always valued. Addressing stereotype threat requires more than just awareness—it needs practical, community-based solutions. For example, local schools could create mentorship programs where students from immigrant backgrounds work with professionals in STEM fields. Volunteering in my community, I've seen how simple activities like math games or coding workshops can help kids feel more confident. But the real challenge is changing the mindset of the adults around them. If teachers and parents don't believe in their potential, it's hard for kids to believe in themselves too. I think we need to focus on building supportive environments where everyone feels safe to try, rather than pushing them to prove they're 'smart enough.' It's not just about fixing the system—it's about making sure that every child, regardless of their background, feels valued and capable.",

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a woman raised in a family where education wasn't prioritized—my father dropped out of school after primary and my mother never finished high school—I've always felt the weight of expectations that I should be 'better' than others to succeed. Stereotype threat in STEM isn't just a theoretical concept; it's something I've seen play out in my own life. When I try to explain my work in sales to my kids, they often ask why I can't do math like their teachers, which makes me anxious because I know that stereotype can hold people back from even trying. In Bologna, where I live with my three children and my partner, I've noticed how many immigrant families struggle with the same thing: they fear their children will be labeled as 'not smart enough' in STEM fields because of their background. This is especially true for me as a Muslim woman who grew up in a country where education wasn't always valued. Addressing stereotype threat requires more than just awareness—it needs practical, community-based solutions. For example, local schools could create mentorship programs where students from immigrant backgrounds work with professionals in STEM fields. Volunteering in my community, I've seen how simple activities like math games or coding workshops can help kids feel more confident. But the real challenge is changing the mindset of the adults around them. If teachers and parents don't believe in their potential, it's hard for kids to believe in themselves too. I think we need to focus on building supportive environments where everyone feels safe to try, rather than pushing them to prove they're 'smart enough.' It's not just about fixing the system—it's about making sure that every child, regardless of their background, feels valued and capable.
